section.title: Let Everything Praise the Lord
section.sectionRationale: The Psalter's final Hallelujah psalm and its crescendo, naming where, why, and how to praise before summoning every living, breathing creature to join the chorus, the fitting climax of a book that moves from lament to unbroken praise, and the goal of all things fulfilled when every creature in heaven and on earth praises God and the Lamb, Revelation 5:13.
section.thesis: The whole Psalter climbs toward this final command, let everything that has breath praise the LORD, a summons fulfilled when all creation joins in unending praise to God and the Lamb.
section.frameNote: Three movements: where and why to praise, verses 1-2; how to praise, with a full orchestra of instruments, verses 3-5; and who is summoned to praise, everything that has breath, verse 6.

verses.text: {{haleluyah|Hallelujah}}! Praise God in His sanctuary. Praise Him in His mighty heavens.
Praise Him for His mighty acts; praise Him for His excellent greatness.

units.label: Praise Him with the sound of the horn
units.note: how to praise, a full orchestra of worship

verses.text: Praise Him with the sound of the horn; praise Him with the harp and lyre.
Praise Him with tambourine and dancing; praise Him with strings and flute.
Praise Him with clashing cymbals; praise Him with resounding cymbals.

units.label: Let everything that has breath praise the Lord
units.note: who is called to praise, the goal of all things

verses.text: Let everything that has {{neshamah|breath}} praise the LORD! {{haleluyah|Hallelujah}}!

haleluyah.gloss: An imperative call, praise the LORD. It opens and closes each of the last five psalms and, here at the very end of the Psalter, brings the whole book's final movement of praise to its crescendo.

neshamah.gloss: Breath, or the breath of life, the same word for the breath God breathed into Adam, Genesis 2:7. Every living creature that breathes owes that breath to God and is summoned here to return it to Him in praise.

basic.context: This is the Psalter's final psalm, its crescendo, naming where praise belongs, in God's sanctuary and His mighty heavens, why it is due, His mighty acts and excellent greatness, and how it should sound, a full orchestra of instruments, before summoning every breathing creature to join.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a hymn of pure, unqualified praise, with no petition, no lament, and no condition attached. Its abiding pattern is that praise belongs to God everywhere, for everything He has done and is, expressed with every gift and voice available, by everyone who lives.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm opens and closes with haleluyah, and its final line summons everything that has neshamah, breath, to praise the LORD, the same breath God breathed into Adam at creation, now called to return to Him as praise.
basic.typology: The universal summons of verse 6, let everything that has breath praise the LORD, anticipates Revelation 5:13's vision of every creature in heaven and on earth, and under the earth and in the sea, singing praise and honor and glory to Him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb.
basic.christ: This psalm's unqualified, universal praise finds its object completed in Christ. Revelation 5:13 shows every creature that has breath, exactly this psalm's summons, finally praising God and the Lamb together, the goal toward which the whole Psalter, and the whole story of redemption, has been moving.
basic.god: God is praised here simply for who He is and what He has done, His mighty acts and His excellent greatness, in the fullness of His dwelling place, both His earthly sanctuary and His mighty heavens, worthy of every instrument and every living voice.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 2:7 describes God breathing the breath of life into Adam, the same breath this psalm calls every creature to return to Him in praise. Revelation 5:13 pictures the fulfillment of this psalm's summons, every creature in heaven and on earth, and in the sea, praising God and the Lamb forever.
basic.oneStory: The Psalter began in the laments and struggles of Book I and climbs, psalm by psalm, toward this final, unbroken crescendo of praise, a summons completed when every creature that has breath, in the new creation, praises God and the Lamb together forever.

context.literary: The last of the Psalter's closing Hallelujah psalms and its final word, built in careful symmetry, a double call in verse 1, a double reason in verse 2, seven instruments named in verses 3-5, and one universal summons in verse 6, mounting toward the book's final Hallelujah.
context.historical: Reflects Israel's temple worship, where sanctuary praise involved trumpet, harp, lyre, tambourine, strings, flute, and cymbals together, Second Temple period practices well attested archaeologically and in texts like 1 Chronicles 15:16.
context.canonical: Functions as the doxological seal on the entire Book of Psalms, gathering every theme, trust, lament, thanksgiving, into a single, final command to praise, and anticipating the unending praise of Revelation 5:13 and 19:1-6.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the whole Psalter to its intended climax, an unqualified, universal call to praise God everywhere, for everything He is and has done, with every instrument and every breathing creature.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm prescribes, more than it describes, a comprehensive pattern of worship, praise offered in every place, for every reason, with every available means, by every living creature, as the fitting response to who God is.

debates.question: Does the psalm's list of instruments, trumpet, harp, lyre, tambourine, strings, flute, cymbals, prescribe specific instruments for corporate worship today?

views.label: The list is descriptive of ancient Israelite practice, not prescriptive of specific instruments for the church
views.case: The psalm's point is comprehensiveness and joy, every available means of the psalmist's own culture; the New Testament nowhere repeats this list as a binding pattern, and different eras and cultures have different instruments to offer.

views.label: The list prescribes an abiding principle of full, unrestrained, embodied worship, whatever instruments are at hand
views.case: The sheer variety and energy of the list, seven different instruments plus dancing, models a standing call to bring every skill and resource available to praise, a principle some argue should shape the fullness of corporate worship in any era.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the specific instruments are not binding in themselves; the abiding principle is that God is worthy of full, wholehearted, and joyful worship, offered with whatever gifts and means His people have.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm opens and closes with haleluyah, praise the LORD, framing its entire crescendo. Its final line summons everything that has neshamah, breath, the same word for the breath of life God breathed into Adam, Genesis 2:7, now summoned to return that gift to Him as praise.

typology.type: The universal summons, let everything that has breath praise the LORD, verse 6
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Revelation 5:13's vision of every creature in heaven and on earth, and under the earth and in the sea, praising God and the Lamb together forever.

study.christ: This psalm's unqualified, universal call to praise finds its object completed in Christ, the Lamb. Revelation 5:13 shows every creature that has breath, exactly this psalm's closing summons, finally praising God and the Lamb together, the fitting goal toward which the whole Psalter, and the whole story of redemption, has been moving from its very first lament to this final, unbroken crescendo.
study.god: God is praised here simply for who He is and what He has done, His mighty acts and His excellent greatness, worshiped in both His earthly sanctuary and His mighty heavens, and worthy of every instrument and every living, breathing voice.

crossReferences.note: Describes God breathing the breath of life into Adam, the same breath, neshamah, this psalm calls every creature to return to Him in praise.
Pictures the fulfillment of this psalm's final summons, every creature in heaven and on earth, and in the sea, praising God and the Lamb forever.

study.oneStory: The Psalter begins in the laments and struggles of Book I and climbs, psalm by psalm, toward this final, unbroken crescendo of praise. That climb is completed when every creature that has breath, in the new creation, praises God and the Lamb together forever, the fitting climax of the whole story of Scripture.

quiz.q: Where does this psalm call for God to be praised, verse 1?

options.text: In His sanctuary and in His mighty heavens.
options.note: The psalm names both the earthly place of worship and the vast heavens as fitting places for praise.

options.text: Only in the wilderness, away from other people.
options.note: The psalm names the sanctuary and the heavens, not isolated wilderness.

options.text: Only in private, never in public gathering.
options.note: The sanctuary points to corporate, gathered worship, not only private devotion.

options.text: Primarily in the heavens, with the sanctuary as an afterthought.
options.note: Both locations are named together with equal weight in verse 1.

quiz.q: Why should God be praised, according to verse 2?

options.text: For His mighty acts and His excellent greatness.
options.note: The psalm grounds praise in both what God has done and who He is.

options.text: Only because it is a religious duty with no real reason.
options.note: The psalm gives specific, substantive reasons, His acts and His greatness.

options.text: Because He needs human praise to be complete.
options.note: The psalm presents praise as a fitting response to God, not a divine need.

options.text: For His mighty acts alone, with no mention of His character.
options.note: The verse names both His acts and His excellent greatness, His character.

quiz.q: How many kinds of instruments are named in verses 3-5?

options.text: Several, horn, harp, lyre, tambourine, strings, flute, and cymbals, a full orchestra of praise.
options.note: The psalm lists a wide and varied range of instruments and dancing.

options.text: Only one, the harp.
options.note: The list is deliberately extensive, not limited to a single instrument.

options.text: None; instruments are forbidden in worship.
options.note: The opposite is true; the psalm actively summons a wide range of instruments.

options.text: Only percussion instruments are named.
options.note: Strings, wind, and percussion instruments are all named together.

quiz.q: Who is called to praise the LORD in verse 6, the psalm's final line?

options.text: Everything that has breath.
options.note: The psalm's closing summons is deliberately universal, excluding no living creature.

options.text: Only the priests of Israel.
options.note: The summons is universal, not restricted to a priestly class.

options.text: Only the angels in heaven.
options.note: The phrase everything that has breath points to earthly, living creatures as well as heavenly beings.

options.text: Only the psalmist himself.
options.note: The final line widens far beyond the psalmist to every breathing creature.

quiz.q: How does Revelation 5:13 relate to this psalm's final summons?

options.text: It pictures the fulfillment, every creature in heaven and on earth, and in the sea, praising Him who sits on the throne and the Lamb forever.
options.note: Revelation 5:13 completes this psalm's universal call with a vision of its final, eternal fulfillment.

options.text: Revelation 5:13 contradicts this psalm's universal call to praise.
options.note: Revelation 5:13 fulfills, rather than contradicts, this psalm's summons.

options.text: The connection is only coincidental wording, with no theological significance.
options.note: The parallel is a deliberate, theologically significant fulfillment, not mere coincidence.

options.text: Revelation 5:13 limits the praise to angels alone, unlike this psalm.
options.note: Revelation 5:13 actually widens the praise further, to every creature, matching this psalm's own universal scope.

questions: This final psalm gives no reason to withhold praise, only reasons to give it. What is keeping you from wholehearted praise to God today?
The psalm calls for praise for His mighty acts and His excellent greatness, verse 2, both what God has done and who He is. Which is easier for you to praise Him for right now, and why?
A full orchestra of instruments is named, verses 3-5. What is your own instrument, your voice, your work, your art, that you can offer in praise this week?
Let everything that has breath praise the LORD includes you, in whatever you are facing today. What would it look like to let your very next breath be an act of praise?
The whole Psalter ends here, in unbroken praise, after so many psalms of lament. How does knowing this is where your own story ends, in praise, change how you carry a current sorrow?

translationNotes.note: His mighty heavens can also be rendered the expanse of His might, or the firmament of His might, echoing the same word used for the sky in Genesis 1:6-8.
Horn renders shofar, a ram's horn trumpet used in worship and as a call to assembly, distinct from the qeren, horn, image of Psalm 148:14.
Breath renders neshamah, the same word for the breath of life God breathed into Adam, Genesis 2:7, tying the psalm's final summons to every living creature.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 150 (https://biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/150.htm) after the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error. Footnote markers [a] and [b] stripped. No em or en dashes present. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg/truthforlife.org, Keller/gospelinlife.com, Piper/desiringgod.org, Ferguson, Carson/thegospelcoalition.org) was found verified for Psalm 150 this session, so sermon status is set to none rather than fabricated.
